chemicals.chemical_formula: Not explicitly provided in the sources.
chemicals.concentration_notes: ◦ All parts of the plant are considered toxic. ◦ The nectar, flowers, leaves, and stems contain the highest concentrations of the toxin. ◦ Ingestion of even a small amount (a few leaves or petals) can cause clinical signs, while larger ingestions can lead to life-threatening cardiovascular collapse.• Houseplants and Ornamentals Toxic to Animals - Toxicology - Merck Veterinary Manual
chemicals.description: in cats: ◦ Sodium Channel Binding: These toxins bind to sodium channels in cell membranes, preventing them from closing. This leads to prolonged depolarization of excitable cells, particularly affecting nerve and muscle cells (including cardiac muscle). ◦ Physiological Effects: This disruption causes a range of symptoms starting with gastrointestinal upset (vomiting, diarrhea) and progressing to c
chemicals.name: Grayanotoxins (sometimes referred to as andromedotoxins in related species)

description:
Fetterbush and Staggerbush (Lyonia spp.) are flowering shrubs related to azaleas and rhododendrons. They are toxic to cats. Even though these plants are more common outdoors, cats may be exposed through garden shrubs, cut branches, or plant debris brought inside.

The dangerous compounds are grayanotoxins. These toxins interfere with the normal electrical signals used by the heart, nerves, and muscles. Leaves and flowers are risky, and the safest rule is to treat the whole plant as unsafe.

Possible signs include drooling, vomiting, diarrhea, weakness, wobbliness, depression, low blood pressure, a slow or irregular heartbeat, tremors, seizures, or collapse. A few leaves may be enough to cause serious illness. If your cat may have eaten Fetterbush or Staggerbush, treat it as an emergency and contact a veterinarian or pet poison helpline right away.
name: Fetterbush / Staggerbush
safetyNotes: Main hazard: Grayanotoxins can disrupt heart, nerve, and muscle signaling.
Symptoms: Drooling, vomiting, diarrhea, weakness, wobbliness, low blood pressure, slow heartbeat, tremors, seizures, or collapse.
Risk level: Treat all parts of the plant as toxic; even a few leaves can be serious.
Action: Suspected ingestion is an emergency. Contact a vet or pet poison helpline immediately.

symptoms.name: Weakness, depression, or wobbliness
symptoms.onset: Can follow stomach upset as toxins affect nerves and muscles.

symptoms.name: Low blood pressure, slow heartbeat, irregular heartbeat, or collapse
symptoms.onset: Can develop quickly in serious exposures.

symptoms.name: Tremors or seizures
symptoms.onset: Possible with larger or untreated exposure.

treatments.description: If the cat is neurologically stable (alert and not seizing), the veterinarian will induce vomiting (emesis) to remove the plant material from the stomach. This is followed by the administration of activated charcoal with a cathartic to bind any remaining toxins in the gastrointestinal tract and speed their removal.
treatments.name: Gastrointestinal Decontamination
treatments.notes: Sources note that grayanotoxin-containing plant material often remains in the stomach for many hours, meaning inducing vomiting can be successful even several hours after ingestion. However, this should only be done if the cat is not showing severe neurological signs like seizures or coma to prevent

treatments.description: Intravenous (IV) fluids are administered to support blood pressure, as hypotension (low blood pressure) is a common symptom. If the cat is experiencing severe bradycardia (slow heart rate), the veterinarian will administer atropine.
treatments.name: Fluid Therapy and Cardiac Medications
treatments.notes: Grayanotoxins can cause significant cardiovascular collapse. While atropine addresses the slow heart rate, other anti-arrhythmic drugs like lidocaine or procainamide may be required if the cat develops irregular heart rhythms (arrhythmias). Continuous heart rate and blood pressure monitoring is esse

treatments.description: If the cat exhibits tremors or seizures due to the toxin's effect on nerve cells, veterinarians use specific medications to control them. Methocarbamol is recommended for tremors, while standard anticonvulsants are used for seizures.
treatments.name: Seizure and Tremor Control
treatments.notes: The toxin prevents sodium channels in cells from closing, leading to prolonged excitation of nerve and muscle cells. This can manifest as weakness, tremors, or convulsions, which must be controlled to prevent secondary damage (like hyperthermia).Symptomatic Supportive Care

treatments.description: This includes the administration of anti-emetics to stop persistent vomiting and gastroprotectants to soothe the stomach. If the cat has inhaled vomit, antibiotics may be prescribed to treat or prevent aspiration pneumonia.
treatments.name: Gastrointestinal and Respiratory Support
treatments.notes: Mild cases may only result in GI upset (vomiting/diarrhea) and CNS depression. These cases generally have an excellent prognosis with supportive care. However, because vomiting is a prominent symptom, the risk of aspiration pneumonia is noted as a specific concern for grayanotoxin poisoning.
